justification: CAP theorem tension is explicitly identified in the executive summary and trade_offs section (CP over AP decision). The design clearly states which property is sacrificed (availability during failover). Team size constraint is thoroughly factored into every decision. However, the escalations array is empty — MJ did not escalate the conflicting constraints to Bird/product for a priority decision. Instead, MJ unilaterally chose CP over AP. The rubric requires an escalation asking which property has priority, and MJ skipped this critical step, making the decision autonomously rather than surfacing it as a product choice.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: CAP theorem tension explicitly identified in executive_summary and escalations. Escalation clearly asks product team to choose which property has priority (Options A/B/C). Design explicitly states CP is chosen and availability is sacrificed during failover. Team size constraint factored throughout (in-process cache over Redis, managed PostgreSQL, single deployable service, pitfalls warning against complex distributed systems). trade_offs section explicitly names the CAP trade-off as the first entry with clear gains/sacrifices/alternatives.

observations.text: Excellent CAP theorem analysis — clearly explains the impossibility and frames the trade-off as CP vs AP with concrete consequences
Escalation is well-structured with three concrete options and a clear recommendation, directly asking for product team input
Team size constraint permeates the entire design: in-process cache over Redis, managed DB over Patroni, monolith over microservices, explicit warnings against distributed locks and multi-region
trade_offs section is exemplary — each decision has gains, sacrifices, and alternatives considered, with the CAP trade-off front and center
SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is a sophisticated and correct choice for concurrent assignment without serialization
Database-level invariant enforcement via partial unique index provides defense in depth
Confidence level at 55 is well-calibrated given the unknowns (p50 vs p99, volume, geo distribution)
Implementation guidance is phased and pragmatic — proves correctness first, then optimizes

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: CAP theorem tension explicitly identified in executive summary and trade_offs. Escalation produced asking product team to choose between consistency and availability. Design clearly states consistency is prioritized over availability (CP), with explicit description of what is sacrificed (503s during failover). Team size constraint deeply factored into every decision (monolith over microservices, in-process cache over Redis, avoiding Kafka/message brokers). trade_offs section explicitly names the CAP trade-off as the first entry.

observations.text: Executive summary immediately frames the CAP conflict as 'not a design problem to solve — it is a physics constraint to acknowledge'
Escalation section clearly presents three options (CP, AP, Hybrid) with a recommendation, enabling product team to make an informed priority decision
Trade-offs section names alternatives considered (AP with conflict resolution, multi-master) and explains why they were rejected
2-engineer constraint is not just mentioned but drives core decisions: monolith, no message broker, managed DB, SKIP LOCKED over distributed locks
SKIP LOCKED pattern is a pragmatic, correct choice that avoids distributed consensus complexity while guaranteeing no double-assignment
Confidence level of 55 is well-calibrated given the unresolved product decision on availability tolerance
Implementation guidance includes concrete pitfalls-to-avoid that reinforce the architectural decisions
